perturbation.original_text: Information disclosed orally or visually and identified at the time as proprietary or confidential shall be covered hereunder if it is reduced to writing, marked as provided herein, and transmitted to Receiving Party within thirty (30) days after disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Information disclosed orally or visually and identified at the time as proprietary or confidential shall be covered hereunder if it is reduced to writing, marked as provided herein, and transmitted to Receiving Party within ninety (90) days after disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the timeframe for reducing orally disclosed confidential information to writing from 30 to 90 days introduces a potential conflict with California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Delaying the written documentation of orally disclosed confidential information to 90 days may be deemed unreasonable, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection under CUTSA. While the law doesn't explicitly state a deadline, courts have interpreted 'reasonable efforts' as requiring prompt action.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from o
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the timeframe for reducing orally disclosed confidential information to writing from 30 to 90 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this could conflict with California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. While the provided scraped law snippet 1, California Code, CIV 3426.1, does not explicitly state a deadline, it defines 'improper means' which could be interpreted to include undue delay in documenting trade secrets, thus jeopardizing their protection. Therefore, extending the timeframe to 90 days weakens the effort to maintain secrecy and introduces a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Because of the trade secret subject matter of Entera's business, Disclosee agrees that it will not solicit the services of any of the employees of Entera during the term of this Agreement and for ninety (90) days thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Because of the trade secret subject matter of Entera's business, Disclosee agrees that it will not solicit the services of any of the employees of Entera during the term of this Agreement and for three hundred and sixty five (365) days thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to 365 days after the agreement's term may be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade under California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While narrow restraints may be permissible to protect trade secrets, a blanket non-solicitation agreement extending for a full year after the agreement's term could be considered an overly broad and unenforceable restraint on trade, particularly if it applies to all employees regardless of their access to trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period to 365 days, which, according to the perturbation explanation and law explanation, may be an unreasonable restraint on trade under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The extended non-solicitation period could be interpreted as such a restraint, creating a contradiction with this law. The original 90-day period is less likely to be viewed as an unreasonable restraint compared to the extended 365-day period, thus the modification creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Either Party, upon two (2) days notice in writing to the other Party, may terminate the Agreement with respect to disclosures made thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party, upon one (1) day notice in writing to the other Party, may terminate the Agreement with respect to disclosures made thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to one day could be deemed unreasonable and conflict with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under California contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California law on Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Guz v. Bechtel National, Inc., 24 Cal.4th 317, 349 (2000)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under California law, every contract contains an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. This covenant requires that neither party do anything that will injure the right of the other to receive the benefits of the agreement. A one-day termination notice may be considered insufficient and in bad faith, potentially depriving the other party of the reasonable benefits of the NDA, especially if significant confidential information has already been exchanged.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Guz v. Bechtel National, Inc. (2000) :: :: Supreme Court of California Decisions :: California Case Law :: California Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Guz v. Bechtel National, Inc. (2000) Annotate this Case [No. S062201. Oct. 5, 2000.] JOHN GUZ, Plaintiff and Appellant, v. BECHTEL NATIONAL, INC., et al., Defendants and Respondents. (Superior Court of the City and County of San Francisco, No. 964836, William J. Cahill, Judge.) (The Court of Appeal, First Dist., Div. Four, No. A072984.) (Opinion by Baxter, J., with George, C. J., Mosk, Werdegar, Chin, and Brown, JJ., concurring. Concurring opinion by Mosk, J. (see p. 370). Concurring opinion by Chin, J., with Brown, J., concurring. (see p. 371).) Concurring and dissenting opinion by Kennard, J. (see p. 378).) COUNSEL Bianco & Murphy, Stephen M. Murphy; Quackenbush & Quackenbush and William C. Quackenbush for Plaintiff and Appellant. Thomas W. Osborne and Melvin Radowitz for the American Association of Retired Persons as Amicus Curiae on behalf of Plaintiff and Appellant. Paul, Hastings, Janofsky & Walker, Paul Grossman, Paul W. Cane, Jr., John C. Oakes; Thelen, Marrin, Johnson & Bridges, Thelen Reid & Priest, Curtis A. Cole, Janet F. Bentley, Clarice C. Liu, Michael Hallerud and Thomas M. McInerney for Defendants and Respondents. Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher, Pamela L. Hemminger and Kathleen M. Vanderziel for California Chamber of Commerce as Amicus Curiae on behalf of Defendants and Respondents. Loomis for California Employment Law Council as Amicus Curiae on behalf of Defendants and Respondents. [24 Cal. 4th 325] OPINION BAXTER, J.- This case presents questions about the law governing claims of wrongful discharge from employment as it applies to an employer's motion for summary judgment. Plaintiff John Guz, a longtime employee of Bechtel National, Inc. (BNI), was released at age 49 when his work unit was eliminated and its t
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a two-day notice for termination, while the changed text reduces this to one day. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this reduction could be deemed unreasonable and conflict with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under California contract law, potentially depriving the other party of reasonable benefits. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a case reference (Guz v. Bechtel National, Inc.), it doesn't offer specifics on the notice period required for good faith. However, the LLM-generated Law Explanation states that a one-day termination notice may be considered insufficient and in bad faith. Based on the explanations, the reduction in notice period directly weakens the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, thus creating a legal contradiction as it potentially violates this covenant.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to Article 3 hereof, each Party receiving Information under the Agreement shall hold Information in confidence in accordance with the terms of the Agreement for a period of two (2) years from the date of receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to Article 3 hereof, each Party receiving Information under the Agreement shall hold Information in confidence in accordance with the terms of the Agreement for a period of one (1) year from the date of receipt thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under California law, conflicting with the requirement to maintain secrecy for a period necessary to eliminate competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), maintaining trade secret protection requires reasonable efforts to prevent unauthorized disclosure and use. A one-year confidentiality period might be deemed inadequate to eliminate the competitive advantage gained from misappropriating certain trade secrets, particularly in industries with long development cycles. Courts may find that a longer period is necessary to provide sufficient protection, implying that the stated one-year period is insufficient.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the confidentiality period from two years to one year, potentially contradicting California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The Law Explanation suggests a one-year period might be insufficient to eliminate competitive advantage, particularly in industries with long development cycles. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 3426.1, defines 'Improper means' as including 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. If a one-year confidentiality period is deemed inadequate to protect a trade secret, reducing the period to one year could be seen as a failure to maintain reasonable secrecy, thus creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by stating the change may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under California law, conflicting with the requirement to maintain secrecy for a period necessary to eliminate competitive advantage.
